title: Chapter 2 (expert) — Asset-free rendering: procedural synthesis
meta.desc: Expert level: runtime texture generation, Web Audio oscillators and envelopes, colour maths (lerp/shade/barrelColor), the animated windsock geometry, particle systems and blend modes, and quality tiers.
hero.title: Asset-free rendering: procedural synthesis
hero.lead: Not a single image or audio file ships. Everything is synthesized: textures baked at boot, sound built from oscillators and envelopes, colours computed by interpolation, geometry generated each frame — and effects scaled to the device.
s1.lead: There are no PNGs. Every particle sprite is drawn once into an off-screen texture at boot, then reused, tinted and blended for the rest of the session.
s1.code.cap: src/systems/textures.js — a soft smoke puff from stacked circles
s1.p1: Four sprites cover the whole game: a hard spark dot, a soft smoke puff, a bright additive flash, and a blobby cloud. White by default, recoloured per biome at runtime.
s1.callout.nerd: Why bake at boot. Generating a texture costs a little CPU once; sampling it costs nothing afterward. We pay the synthesis price a single time, then enjoy GPU-cheap, tintable sprites — with zero download.
s2.p1: Every sound effect is a tiny Web Audio graph: a source (an oscillator or a buffer of noise), a filter, and a gain envelope that shapes the volume over time. An explosion is filtered noise whose low-pass sweeps down while the gain decays.
s2.p2: Richer effects just stack graphs: the tower rubble() layers a low rumble, a deep sine thud, and a scatter of short high-passed “debris” bursts at random times. The continuous flyby() whizz keeps one band-passed sawtooth alive and nudges its gain and pitch toward a target with setTargetAtTime as a shell nears. On iOS, where the Vibration API is blocked, this audio whizz stands in for the haptics you'd otherwise feel.
s2.callout.suspended: The suspended context. Browsers create the AudioContext suspended and only resume it after a user gesture. A single shared Sfx instance lives on the game registry and is unlock()-ed on first interaction — otherwise the opening sounds vanish silently.
s2.callout.tip: Remember. A synthesized sound = source + filters + envelope. We store no samples: we describe a recipe the browser runs. The bundle stays tiny.
s3.p1: Colours are computed, not stored. Linear interpolation (lerp) between two RGB triples drives the charging barrel (steel → glowing red), and a multiplicative shade brightens or darkens any colour. All of it lives in the renderer-agnostic src/render/visuals.js.
s3.p2: barrelColor(power) lerps from cool steel toward burning red on a power ratio clamped to [0, 1]; craterRimColor(edge) pulls the biome's edge tint toward dark brown for a “scorched earth” rim rather than a black ring. The bit-shifts (>> 16, & 0xff) extract each channel from a 0xRRGGBB integer.
s3.callout.nerd: For the curious. Colours are packed integers (0xRRGGBB). Bit-shifting splits the channels, the maths runs per channel, and the shift-OR repacks them — the same representation Phaser and a plain canvas both accept, which is why this code is shared.
s4.p1: The windsock isn't a sprite — it's geometry generated every frame. From the base point, the wind strength and the time, computeWindsock returns a pole plus tapering striped quads that point downwind, lift with strength, and flutter with a time-driven sine.
s4.p2: Because it returns plain points and colours, the TV draws it with Phaser graphics and the controller draws the same shape on a 2D canvas — one definition, two renderers.
s5.p1: Ambient scenery is a single emitter configured by data. Each biome picks a behaviour — drifting leaves, falling snow, rising embers, blowing sand — and embers add an ADD blend mode so they glow. The live wind even bends the stream by setting the emitter's gravity.
s5.code.c4: // additive: bright embers glow over the dark sky
s5.p2: All this synthesis can overwhelm a smart TV. So at boot the host samples real frame deltas for a short window, computes the average fps, and records a tier the renderer respects (fewer clouds, no ambient layer on lite).
s5.code2.cap: src/objects/Background.js — quality decides what we draw
s5.code2.c1: // the ambient layer is the easiest to sacrifice on a weak device
s5.callout.tip: Describe behaviour, not particles. We never spawn each flake by hand. We hand the engine rules — speed, lifespan, start/end size and alpha — and it produces hundreds of particles that obey them, the wind shaping the whole stream at once.
